coredata.dc:title: Ultraefficient electrochemical biosensing platform utilizing aloe vera-derived carbon nanodots for ANXA2 liver cancer biomarker diagnosis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kulkarni, Utkarsha Sachin
Gawali, Chetan Ramesh
Pankaj,
Kumar, Suveen
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of nanomaterials from natural resources offers a cost-effective and environmentally sustainable approach for biosensor development. In this study, carbon nanodot (CND)-based electrochemical biosensor has been developed for the early diagnosis of liver cancer (LC) biomarker i.e. ANXA2. Initially, Aloe vera has been chosen as the carbon precursor for the synthesis of CNDs. The synthesis of carbon nanodots began with the extraction of Aloe vera gel, which has been mixed with ultrapure water and subjected to hydrothermal processing at 180 °C for 12 h. Then the obtained product has been filtered through 0.22 µm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane, washed with dichloromethane and stored at 4 °C. The size of synthesized CNDs has been obtained as ∼5.6 nm. Further, the prepared CNDs have been electrophoretically deposited onto hydrolysed indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass electrodes under an optimized potential of 10 V for 60 s at 300 mA. Further, the obtained thin film of CNDs onto the ITO electrode has been used to immobilize anti-ANXA2 via drop casting method and bovine serum albumin (BSA) has been used for the blocking of non-specific binding sites. The morphological, structural and electrochemical properties of the synthesized CNDs and the fabricated electrodes have been characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), attenuated total reflectance (ATR), UV-Vis spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. Further the fabricated BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO biosensing platform has been used to investigate the ANXA2 biomarker via CV technique and the obtained results indicate the fabricated platform shows liner detection range in between 1 fg mL⁻¹ to 10 ng mL⁻¹ and 10–500 ng mL⁻¹, sensitivities 1.36 µA [log (ng mL⁻¹)]⁻¹ cm⁻² and 6.84 µA [log (ng mL⁻¹)]⁻¹ cm⁻² respectively with response time of 20 min. Notably, the biosensor demonstrated excellent selectivity with minimal interference from common biological analytes present in the serum sample of liver cancer patients with highly reproducible in nature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot
Liver cancer
Electrochemical
Biosensor
Nanomaterial

National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Ahmedabad

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to Director, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Ahmedabad for providing the necessary facilities to carry out this work. We are also thankful to the Centre for Medical Devices, NIPER-Ahmedabad for providing basic infrastructure to conduct the experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD spectra of CND/ITO electrode, (b) TEM image, (c) HR-TEM image and (d) SAED pattern of synthesized CND.
ATR spectra of fabricated CND/ITO and anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrodes.

a) Scan rate investigation in the range (10 mV s−1- 200 mV s−1), (b) plot of Ipa and Ipc versus square root of scan rate, (c) plot of ΔEp versus square root of scan rate of CND/ITO electrode, (d) Scan rate investigation in the range (10 mV s−1- 200 mV s−1), (e) plot of Ipa and Ipc versus square root of scan rate, (f) plot of ΔEp versus square root of scan rate of BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode.

(a) Electrochemical response study of BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode versus various concentration of ANXA2 (1 fg mL−1 - 500 ng mL−1) via CV technique, (b) magnified view of Ipa versus different concentration of ANXA2 (1 fg mL−1 - 500 ng mL−1), (c) calibration plot between obtained peak current at different concentration of ANXA2 through BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode versus log [Concentration of ANXA2 (ng mL−1)], (d) control studies of BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode vs ANXA2 concentration (1 fg mL−1 - 500 ng mL−1), (e) interferent studies of BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO immunoelectrode versus interferents present in healthy human serum sample and (f) Reproducibility studies of four different immunoelectrodes (BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO) fabricated via same strategy.
Comparison between obtained biosensing parameters of present work with previous work.

row.entry: 1 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1 and 10 – 500 ng mL−1
1.36 µA [log (ng mL−1)]−1 cm−2 and 6.84 µA [log (ng mL−1)]−1 cm−2

head.ce:title: Ultraefficient electrochemical biosensing platform utilizing aloe vera-derived carbon nanodots for ANXA2 liver cancer biomarker diagnosis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Devices, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research-Ahmedabad, Gujarat 382355, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medical Devices, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research-Ahmedabad

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medical Devices, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of nanomaterials from natural resources offers a cost-effective and environmentally sustainable approach for biosensor development. In this study, carbon nanodot (CND)-based electrochemical biosensor has been developed for the early diagnosis of liver cancer (LC) biomarker i.e. ANXA2. Initially, Aloe vera has been chosen as the carbon precursor for the synthesis of CNDs. The synthesis of carbon nanodots began with the extraction of Aloe vera gel, which has been mixed with ultrapure water and subjected to hydrothermal processing at 180 °C for 12 h. Then the obtained product has been filtered through 0.22 µm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane, washed with dichloromethane and stored at 4 °C. The size of synthesized CNDs has been obtained as ∼5.6 nm. Further, the prepared CNDs have been electrophoretically deposited onto hydrolysed indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass electrodes under an optimized potential of 10 V for 60 s at 300 mA. Further, the obtained thin film of CNDs onto the ITO electrode has been used to immobilize anti-ANXA2 via drop casting method and bovine serum albumin (BSA) has been used for the blocking of non-specific binding sites. The morphological, structural and electrochemical properties of the synthesized CNDs and the fabricated electrodes have been characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), attenuated total reflectance (ATR), UV-Vis spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. Further the fabricated BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO biosensing platform has been used to investigate the ANXA2 biomarker via CV technique and the obtained results indicate the fabricated platform shows liner detection range in between 1 fg mL⁻¹ to 10 ng mL⁻¹ and 10–500 ng mL⁻¹, sensitivities 1.36 µA [log (ng mL⁻¹)]⁻¹ cm⁻² and 6.84 µA [log (ng mL⁻¹)]⁻¹ cm⁻² respectively with response time of 20 min. Notably, the biosensor demonstrated excellent selectivity with minimal interference from common biological analytes present in the serum sample of liver cancer patients with highly reproducible in nature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: From last several decades nanomaterials play an important role in various sectors including agriculture, diagnostics, energy, solar cell etc. due to their efficient properties in terms of conductivity, mechanical strength, solubility, biocompatibility etc (Wang, 2005). In various field of diagnosis, nanomaterials have been widely used in the development of electrochemical biosensors where it has been used as an immobilization matrix for bioreceptors (Zhu et al., 2015; Daweshar, Pankaj et al., 2024). This immobilization matrix helps in enhancing the electrochemical signals generated during the binding of analytes with the biosensing platform (Kumar et al., 2023a, 2016a). Keeping these in view various efforts have been made towards the use of nanomaterials in the fabrication of biosensing platforms like metal oxide (HfO2, MgO, ZrO2, Al2O3etc.) (Baranwal et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2016a; Shankar et al., 2025), metal chalcogenides (ZrS3, CaTe, Cu2Se etc.) (Won et al., 2013; Shankar et al., 2023a), Mxenes etc. However, their synthesis is one of the major challenges like control synthesis (in terms of shape and size), reproducibility and its toxic effect on the environment. For this mainly chemical as well as physical process has been used but use of chemical as precursor/source for nanomaterial synthesis is one of the major challenges not only for synthesis prospective but also for environment concern (Shankar et al., 2023b; Pechyen et al., 2024). To overcome these challenges, efforts have been made to use natural products like Aloe vera, neem, sunflower etc. for the synthesis of nanomaterials (Malode et al., 2023; Srivastava et al., 2024; Raji et al., 2022). Metal oxide and metal chalcogenides offers some disadvantage such as low conductivity, low mass/charge transfer and also diminishes capacity at high current density while carbon nanomaterial like Carbon dots (CDs) have shown unique electrical as well as optoelectronic properties along with other characteristics such as superior conductivity, dispersibility in various solvents, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, etc (Hassanvand et al., 2021). Conventionally, the development of carbon dot from chemical substance as precursor that are toxic, expensive, required additional techniques etc. while now a days, the development of nanomaterial from natural products provide numerous advantages such as inexpensive, biodegradable, eco-friendly, etc (Vernekar et al., 2021). For instance, Yang et al. synthesized carbon dots from glucose molecules in the presence of monopotassium phosphate via hydrothermal approach (Campuzano et al., 2019). In another report, Mehta et al. first synthesized the carbon dot nanoparticles from apple juice by hydrothermal approach and employed as fluorescent probe in imaging the mycobacterium and fungal cells (Yang et al., 2011). The other reported synthesis of carbon dots from biowaste materials such as grape skin and kiwi peels by one step hydrothermal method for detection of picric acid (Mehta et al., 2015) and for imaging human normal cells along with cancer cells respectively (Li et al., 2018). Keeping this consideration in mind, we focus on the synthesis of carbon dot from Aloe vera as precursor. The Aloe vera is readily available natural carbon rich resource that possesses some excellent characteristics such as high hierarchical porous and high dispersion that facilitate fast transfer of charge in the redox process (Atchudan et al., 2022).

Liver cancer (LC) has the third mortality rate (8.3 million in 2020, WHO report) among the other cancer across worldwide (Malavika et al., 2021). The risk factors such as smoking, drinking habit of alcohol, exposure of aflatoxin B1 due to food toxin, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease etc that associated with the occurrence of liver cancer (Chauhan et al., 2022a; Chuang et al., 2009). The conventional techniques such as CT scans, biopsy, ultrasound, laparoscopy, MRI test, AFP serum tests etc. are utilized for the detection of liver cancer (Klingenberg et al., 2017). These conventional techniques have several limitations such as require tissue samples, expensive, radiation exposure, sophisticated instruments, and time-consuming process in liver cancer detection. Additionally, the liver cancer cannot be diagnosed at early stage using these kinds of techniques if the size of the lesion greater than 3 cm (Bialecki and Di Bisceglie, 2005). Therefore, there is rapid need of detection technique that can detect the liver cancer at early stage and can overcome these limitations to mitigate the impact. In this regard, the detection of liver cancer biomarker via electrochemical biosensor shows numerous potentials such as fast response, user-friendly, highly sensitive, minimal invasive etc (Kumar et al., 2019; Chauhan et al., 2023; Nirbhaya et al., 2022). Various biomarkers of liver cancer such as AFP, DCP, GPC3, AFU, etc. that are explored by researcher so far. It was reported that Annexin A2 (ANXA2) biomarker is more sensitive, specific, and predictive in clinical cases of liver cancer than most commonly biomarker like AFP. ANXA2 is a member of the Annexin superfamily having a molecular weight 36 kDa. It can bind to calcium and phospholipids and is present on the outer surface of endothelial cells as well as various tumor cells (Li et al., 2018). It was found that Annexin A2 was significantly higher in the blood of LC patients compared to healthy individuals. At a cutoff level (∼18 ng mL−1) that increases over 500 ng mL−1 in patients suffering from LC, this marker had 86–100 % sensitivity and 84–91 % specificity, with a diagnostic accuracy of 92 % (El-Abd et al., 2016; Herrera-López et al., 2023). Therefore, ANXA2 biomarker show excellent parameters and not usually utilized for diagnosis of LC. Therefore, the ANXA2 has been utilized as biomarker for development of biosensor towards LC diagnosis in this work.

In present work, the carbon dot was firstly synthesised from natural precursor (Aloe vera) via hydrothermal method. To our knowledge, the present work is the first report toward the synthesis of carbon dot from Aloe vera and then employed as immobilization matrix for biosensing platform towards detection of liver cancer biomarker. This biosensor has the potential for efficient detection of liver cancer ANXA2 biomarker in concentration ranging from 1 fg mL−1 to 10 ng mL−1 and 10 g mL−1 to 500 ng mL−1 with corresponding sensitivity of 1.36 µA [log (ng mL−1)]−1 cm−2 and 6.84 µA [log (ng mL−1)]−1 cm−2 respectively.

Dichloromethane (CH₂Cl₂) and Dimethylformamide [HCON(CH3)2] were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific India Pvt. Ltd. Ammonia solution (NH3; 30 %) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2; 50 %) were purchased from SDFCL and EMPLURA, respectively. Phosphate monobasic monohydrate (NaH2PO4·H2O), sodium phosphate dibasic heptahydrate (Na₂HPO4·7H2O), magnesium nitrate (Mg (NO3)2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), sodium chloride (NaCl) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. ITO coated glass electrodes were obtained from Optoelectronics. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was prepared using the Milli-Q system (ELIX Synergy UV-R). Antigens and antibodies of ANXA2 were obtained from Sigma Lifescience. Aloe vera leaves were freshly collected from the NIPER-Ahmedabad, Gujarat (India). All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The structural properties of the synthesized nanomaterial were investigated using X-ray diffractometer (XRD; D8 FOCUS BRUKER) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; THEMIS) was employed to analyze the morphological structure. The functional groups present in the nanomaterial and fabricated electrodes were identified using Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR; BRUKER ALPHA). An electrophoretic deposition (EPD) unit (GenBiotech MP-300V) was utilized to deposit nanomaterials onto indium tin oxide (ITO) electrodes. Electrochemical characterizations and studies were carried out using an Autolab Potentiostat/Galvanostat (AUT204, Netherlands), with a three-electrodes system comprising Ag/AgCl as reference electrode, modified ITO electrode as working electrode, and platinum wire as counter electrode, spaced 1 cm apart with each other. All experiments were conducted using the NOVA software integrated with the Autolab system. All electrochemical studies were performed three times.

The synthesis of carbon nanodots began with the collection of Aloe vera leaves from the NIPER-Ahmedabad campus. The leaves were peeled to extract the gel, which was chopped into small pieces and weighed. A total of 200 g of gel was mixed with ultrapure water to prepare a 1000 mL solution. From this, 130 mL was transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. After completion of this, the mixture was allowed to cool naturally for 5 h. The cooled product was filtered using a 0.22 µm PTFE membrane filtration assembly to remove impurities, followed by washing with dichloromethane (DCM). The purified filtrate was then stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The ITO-coated glass electrodes were pre-hydrolyzed by initially washing with ultrapure water and followed by isopropyl alcohol (IPA). Next, the cleaned and dried ITO electrodes were immersed in a freshly prepared solution consisting of ammonia (NH3), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and ultrapure water in a 1:1:5 ratio. The electrodes were then incubated at 80 °C for 1 h in a hot air oven. After incubation, the oxidizing solution was carefully discarded, and the electrodes were rinsed alternately with ultrapure water and IPA solvent three times. The hydrolyzed ITO electrodes were then dried and stored in a desiccator until further use.

For the preparation of thin film of CND, solution was prepared by mixing 1 mL of synthesized nanodot solution of Optical Density: 2.86 (data shown in Figure S1) with 13 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF), followed by bath sonication for 10 min. To this solution, 50 μL of magnesium nitrate was added to enhance the charges on the CND that helps in the formation of thin film onto the ITO electrode. Further, a two-electrodes system was employed for the EPD process with an ITO electrode as the deposition substrate and platinum wire as the reference electrode. The optimized parameters for the deposition of uniform thin film were 10 V, 300 mA and 60 s. The deposited electrodes were carefully stored in a desiccator until further use.

The anti-ANXA2 solution was immobilized onto the fabricated CND/ITO electrode via physical adsorption. For this, 30 μL of the anti-ANXA2 solution (50 µg/mL) was dropped onto the thin film, ensuring that the entire solution adhered to the film. After 2 h, the film was washed three times with PBS. Next, in order to inhibit nonspecific sites of ANXA2 antibodies, film was incubated with BSA (2 mg dL−1) for 30 min, followed by three additional washes with PBS (pH=7.0). The antibody functionalized electrode ANXA2/CND/ITO was then stored at 4 °C for future studies.

A 100 mL synthetic serum sample was prepared by accurately weighing and dissolving 0.68 g sodium chloride, 0.02 g calcium chloride, 0.04 g potassium chloride, 0.01 g magnesium sulfate, 0.22 g sodium bicarbonate, 0.0126 g disodium hydrogen phosphate, and 0.0026 g sodium dihydrogen phosphate in ultrapure water. The solution was stirred for 1 h on a magnetic stirrer at room temperature to ensure complete homogenization. The prepared sample was then stored at 4 °C for stability. Subsequently, antigen dilutions in the range of 1 fg mL⁻¹ to 500 ng mL⁻¹ were prepared using this synthetic serum as the matrix (Raoufi et al., 2007).

The crystallinity of synthesized carbon nanodots (CNDs) derived from Aloe vera gel extract were examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD). Since the synthesized CND was obtained in a dispersed form, a uniform layer of the material was prepared onto the ITO electrode via the drop-casting approach for XRD analysis. The resulting XRD pattern is shown in Fig. 1a which exhibits diffraction peaks at 18.81° and 33.01°, that corresponds to the (002) and (222) planes respectively (Pechyen et al., 2024; Herrera-López et al., 2023). It was noted that peak at 18.81° is attributed to presence of carbon nanomaterial in its amorphous form, derived from Aloe vera which is in accordance with JCPDS card No. 41–1487. The other peak observed at 33.01° corresponds to the ITO glass substrate. Thus, the obtained XRD results confirms the successful synthesis of CNDs.

To investigate the morphological studies of synthesized CND, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed. As can be seen in obtained images (Fig. 1b and Fig. 1c), it has been observed that the synthsized nanomaterial is spherical in shape with average size of ∼5.6 nm and the corresponding SAED pattern is depicted in Fig. 1d. Therefore, from the obtained TEM data term nanodot (CND) was used for the synthesized carbon nanomaterial.

To examine the functional groups on the CND/ITO and anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrodes, ATR spectroscopy was performed and the obtained results are presented in Fig. 2. The ATR spectrum of the CND/ITO electrode exhibited characteristic peaks at 1703 cm⁻¹ , 1649 cm⁻¹ , and 1437 cm⁻¹ corresponding to CO stretching vibrations, CC aromatic stretching and C–O bending vibrations of CND respectively. These peaks were also observed in spectra of anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode, indicating that underlying CND/ITO structure remains consistent after antibody immobilization. In addition to these common peaks, the spectrum of the anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode displayed distinctive peaks at 1554 cm⁻¹ and 1512 cm⁻¹

which correspond to Amide II vibrations, and at 1273 cm⁻¹ is attributed to C–N stretching vibrations (Purohit et al., 2019). Furthermore, the appearance of broad O–H stretching vibrations in the range of 3300–3400 cm⁻¹ in the anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode spectrum highlights the proteinaceous nature of the immobilized layer. These spectral observations collectively validate the effective immobilization of anti-ANXA2 onto the CND/ITO electrode.

In order to conduct the electrochemical characterization and response studies, a three-electrodes system has been employed. Initially, to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of the ferri-ferrocyanide redox couple ([Fe(CN)6]3-/4-) onto the ITO electrode surface, CV has been performed across a potential range in between −0.8 V to + 0.8 V at an optimized scan rate of 50 mV s−1. For this experiment, different analytical solutions have been used. In one analytical solution we have used 0.2 M PBS buffer however in another solution 0.2 M PBS buffer containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- has been used with neutral pH. The obtained results are presented in Figure S2. The presence of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- resulted in distinct oxidation and reduction peaks, confirming the electrochemical activity of the probe. Conversely, in the absence of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, no discernible redox current has been detected, indicating that the analytical solution with [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- is essential for facilitating electron transfer at the electrode surface. This observation also suggests that, [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- plays a crucial role in amplifying peak current in electrochemical studies, highlighting its significance as a redox probe for evaluating electrode behaviour. Based on this finding, subsequent electrochemical measurements were conducted in 0.2 M PBS containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- to ensure optimal electron transfer conditions.

Furthermore, the impact of pH on the electrochemical response of the developed electrode (BSA/anti-ANXA2/CNDs/ITO) has been assessed via CV at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, and the obtained results are shown in Figure S3. The variation in peak current of analytical solution with different pH has been examined, revealing the highest peak current at pH 7.0. This observation is attributed to the stabilization of biomolecules in their native state at neutral pH (7.0), where their biological activity is maximized. In contrast, exposure to acidic (<pH 7.0) or basic (>pH 7.0) environments may induce biomolecular denaturation, leading to diminished biological activity and reduced electrochemical response. Hence, PBS at pH 7.0 has been selected as the optimal buffer for further studies.

To evaluate the kinetics and charge transfer mechanisms of the modified electrodes (CND/ITO and BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO), scan rate studies were conducted. The electrochemical responses of both electrodes have been analysed over a scan rate range of 10 mV s−1 to 200 mV s−1 via CV technique, and the obtained results are depicted in Fig. 3. With an increase in scan rate, the anodic and cathodic peak currents progressively shifted towards more positive and more negative potentials, respectively, signifying quasi-reversible kinetics (Fig. 3a and 3d). Additionally, a linear relationship was observed (shown in Fig. 3b and 3e) between the peak currents (Ipc or Ipa) and the square root of the scan rate, confirming that the electron transfer process follows diffusion-controlled kinetics. This behaviour is characteristic of redox reactions involving surface-bound electroactive species and supports the assumption that electron transfer occurs via a diffusion-limited mechanism (Kennepohl et al., 2016). which adheres to Eqs. (i-iv):
(i) Ipc(CND/ITO) = [(4.15±0.04) µA(smV−1) x (scan rate[mVs−1])1/2] + (-0.049±0.4) µA, R2 = 0.99
(ii) Ipa(CND/ITO) = -[(3.73±0.0) µA(smV−1) x (scan rate[mVs−1])1/2] – (-0.53±0.25) µA, R2 = 0.99
(iii) Ipc(BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO) = [(22.82±0.0) µA(smV−1) x (scan rate[mVs−1])1/2] + (-3.82±0.3) µA, R2 = 0.99
(iv) Ipc(BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO) = -[(18.44±0.1) µA(smV−1) x (scan rate[mVs−1])1/2] - (-18.10±1.4) µA, R2 = 0.99

Moreover, the anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) peak potentials difference (ΔEp=Epa-Epc) for both CND/ITO and BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrodes displayed a linear relationship (shown in Fig. 3c and 3f) with the square root of the scan rate (10–200 mV s−1), as described by Eqs. (v) and (vi) . This indicates facile transfer of electron from the solution to the electrode surface:
(v) ΔEp(V)CND/ITO = [(7.88±0.0) V(smV−1) x (scan rate[mVs−1])1/2] + (0.04±0.0) V, R2 = 0.99
(vi) ΔEp(V)BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO = [(41.26±0.1) V(smV−1) x (scan rate[mVs−1])1/2] + (14.27±1.3) V, R2 = 0.99

The response of developed electrodes at various steps i.e. CND/ITO, anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO and BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO has been investigated electrochemically using CV technique and the obtained results are shown in Figure S4. The measurements were examined at optimized scan rate (50 mVs−1) in a 0.2 M PBS solution (pH 7.0) containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)₆]³ ⁻/⁴⁻. The cyclic voltammograms revealed distinct changes in redox peak currents corresponding to each modification stage. The CND/ITO electrode exhibited well-defined anodic and cathodic peaks; indicative of efficient electron transfers due to the high conductivity of the CND/ITO surface. Following the immobilization of anti-ANXA2 antibodies, a notable increase in both anodic and cathodic peak currents was observed. This increase can be ascribed to the presence of free NH3 + group of antibodies molecules that facilitate non-covalent interaction (Chauhan et al., 2022b; Kumar et al., 2023b; Basiaga et al., 2014).

In prior to conduct electrochemical response studies, response time study has been conducted. In these experiments to investigate the binding time required to interact analytes (ANXA2) present in electrolytic solution and anti-ANXA2 immobilized into the fabricated biosensing electrode i.e. BSA/ anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO, incubation time study have been performed. For this we have added 20 µL of ANXA2 into the analytical/ electrolytic solution and further takes its CV response with a regular interval of 5 min and up to 30 min. We observed that, from 0 to 20 min, current has been increased and after that there is no change in peak current up to 30 min, indicating time required to bind ANXA2 biomarker present in analytical solution to the fabricated electrode is 20 min. So, we have provided 20 min as incubation time before conducting electrochemical response studies of fabricated electrode against each concentration of ANXA2 protein.

Next, response studies of BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode have been conducted toward the detection of different concentration of ANXA2 antigen through CV technique. The electrochemical investigations have been conducted within potential range (-0.8 V to + 0.8 V) at 50 mV s−1. For the study of electrode response, 20 µL of each concentration of ANXA2 protein (1 fg mL−1 to 500 ng mL−1) has been added into the analytical solution and measured its peak current via CV technique. From the obtained result (as shown in Fig. 4a and 4b), we observed that an increase in the anodic peak current with each addition of ANXA2 protein (1 fg mL−1 to 500 ng mL−1). These observed increase in peak current with respect to each addition of different concentration of ANXA2 is attributed to the specific binding interactions between the ANXA2 antigen and the immobilized anti-ANXA2 antibodies on the BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode surface. These interactions likely enhanced electron transfer efficiency, reflected as an amplified current response in the presence of the antigen (Kumar et al., 2024). Observed increase in current during sensing studies. The physical immobilization of the antibody onto the CND/ITO surface, followed by sensing studies conducted onto the BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO system, indicates that the observed increase in current may be attributed to conformational changes in the ANXA2 protein induced by immunoreactions between anti-ANXA2 and ANXA2. To further substantiate this mechanism, we retrieved the amino acid sequence of ANXA2 from the NCBI protein database (accession: BAA00013.1) (Kumar et al., 2016b). The sequence analysis revealed the presence of four cysteine (C) residues, among which Cys-8 and Cys-334 are located adjacent to positively charged amino acid fragments, rendering them highly reactive. During the electrochemical process, these cysteine residues undergo oxidation, facilitating electron transfer and thereby contributing to the observed increase in peak current. Additionally, the progressive increase in amino acid content due to the rising concentration of ANXA2 enhances antigen-antibody interactions, leading to a further increase in current response. This mechanistic insight provides a strong rationale for the increasing slope observed in our sensing studies (Kennepohl et al., 2016).

Along with these, a calibration curve has been plotted (as shown in Fig. 4c) in between Ipa observed after each addition of different concentration of ANXA2 and the log of concentration of ANXA2. The calibration plot gave estimated sensitivity values of 1.36 µA [log (ng mL−1)]−1 cm−2 (for concentrations ranging from 1 fg mL−1 to 10 ng mL−1) and 6.84 μA [log (ng mL−1)]−1cm−2 (for concentrations ranging from10 ng mL−1 to 500 ng mL−1) and follows the Eqs. (vii) and (viii) :
(vii) I = [0.34± 0.033 µA [log (ng mL−1)]−1 × log [concentration of ANXA2 (ng mL−1)] + [25.52± 0.24] µA, R2 = 0.96
(viii) I = [1.71± 0.075 µA [log (ng mL−1)]−1 × log [concentration of ANXA2 (ng mL−1)] + [21.80± 0.34] µA, R2 = 0.99

This immunosensor demonstrated a wider LDR of 1 fg mL−1 to 500 ng mL−1 for ANXA2, aligning with its physiological concentration range, which distinguishes normal individuals from liver cancer patients. Moreover, this fabricated biosensor outperforms other biosensors reported so far such as MWCNT, PEG/Au/PANI, PANI-BSA-Ag etc. in terms of this wider LDR for liver cancer detection. Along with this the fabricated BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO biosensor provides substantially lower LOD of 1 fg mL−1, indicating the developed biosensor having the ability to detect ANXA2 biomarker more efficiently. Table 1 compares the analytical characteristics of the fabricated electrode to those of sensors that have already been published.

To investigate the obtained electrochemical response is due to specific binding between anti-ANXA2 present onto the BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO with ANXA2, control studies have been conducted. For this the different concentrations of the ANXA2 antigen (10–500 ng mL−1) were added onto CND/ITO electrode under identical experimental conditions as those employed in electrochemical response studies. The obtained results indicated no significant variation (avg. %RSD <5 %) in the anodic peak current with increasing concentrations of ANXA2 antigen, suggesting the absence of interaction between the antigen and the CND/ITO electrode (as shown in Fig. 4 e). This confirms that the electrochemical response observed in electrochemical response was due to the specific binding of the ANXA2 antigen with the binding onto the anti-ANXA2 antibodies present on BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO immunoelectrode.

To evaluate cross-reactivity, interference studies were conducted using the developed BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode with various serum analytes, including urea (1 mg mL−1), glucose (7 mg mL−1), sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (10 mg mL−1), ammonium sulfate (0.3 mg mL−1), sodium citrate (5 µg mL−1), ascorbic acid (0.24 µg mL−1), sodium chloride (20 mM), calcium chloride (8.38 mM), and potassium chloride (8.38 mM) (as shown in Fig. 4f). The obtained results showed negligible changes in response (% RSD 4.2 %) with the addition of these interferents, suggesting that BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode exhibits high selectivity for detection of ANXA2. This selectivity is attributed to specific antigen-antibody interactions taking place on BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO biosensing platform.

Next, in order to check the reproducibility nature of developed BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO immunoelectrode, reproducibility investigations have been performed. The four different BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO immunoelectrodes have been developed under identical parameters and their CV response has been noted for reproducibility studies and depicted in Fig. 4f. It has been observed that insignificant change in response with average % RSD is ∼ 3.7 %, that is under acceptable range, signifies that fabricated BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO electrode is highly reproducible in nature.

In this study, Aloe vera-derived carbon nanodots (CNDs) were successfully synthesized via the hydrothermal route and successively utilized for the fabrication of a highly efficient electrochemical biosensing platform i.e. BSA/anti-ANXA2/CND/ITO for the detection of liver cancer ANXA2 biomarker. The electrochemical performance of the immunosensor has been evaluated and observed two distinct linear detection ranges i.e. 1 fg mL⁻¹ to 10 ng mL⁻¹ and 10 ng mL⁻¹ to 500 ng mL⁻¹ , with remarkable sensitivities of 1.36 µA [log (ng mL⁻¹)] ⁻¹ cm⁻² and 6.84 µA [log (ng mL⁻¹)] ⁻¹ cm⁻², respectively. The fabricated platform also shows rapid detection with response time of 20 min and shelf life of 45 days. These findings show the potential of Aloe vera-derived CNDs as cost-effective and environmentally friendly nanomaterials towards the fabrication of advanced biosensor. The synthesized bio-based nanomaterials open a new window towards its use of smart and sustainable biosensor development. It may also use in the other biomedical applications like gene/drug delivery, tissue engineering, bioimaging etc.

Kulkarni Utkarsha Sachin: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Gawali Chetan Ramesh: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Kumar Suveen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Pankaj: Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Director, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Ahmedabad for providing the necessary facilities to carry out this work. We are also thankful to the Centre for Medical Devices, NIPER-Ahmedabad for providing basic infrastructure to conduct the experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2025.120996.

Commun. 47 (2011) 11615–11617. https://doi.org/10.1039/c1cc14860e.
